3: Achieving a sustainable society requires a transition from fossil- based to renewable-based energy system. Global warming due to human activity is mainly a result of fossil fuel use. Global CO2 emissions due to human activities have increased by 2.2% per year between 2005 and 2015. To limit the global average temperature increase to less than 2% (a goal of the Paris Agreement), CO2 emissions needs to be reduced [1].

Renewable energy use has grown significantly in the power gener- ation sector [2]. By 2040, its electricity generation share will triple because of new policies and make up more than 40% of overall elec- tricity generation [2]. There are some concerns regarding the sustain- ability of biofuels when feedstock can also be a source of food (i.e., biofuels can not only compete with food production for agricultural land

but their production can also lead to land clearing and cultivation, which increase in CO2 emissions) [2]. Currently, biomass residues/by-products are treated as waste and are underused. A bio-based economy can be a pillar of a sustainable society wherein biomass residues/wastes or organic wastes are sources of clean energy and products such as high-value bio-based chemicals, fuels, and elec- tricity [3].

The global share of bioenergy in the primary energy mix is 13%, more than any other renewable source [4]. However, biomass on its own has some disadvantages such as low mass and energy densities, low heating value, and non-homogeneity; poor grindability, hydrophilicity, seasonality and storage stability; and high volatile, oxygen, and mois- ture content, all of which make its handling, transportation, and use difficult and costly [5-7].
Dry torrefaction (DT) and novel wet torrefaction (WT) processes are

two possible ways of converting residual biomass feedstocks to bio-coal that eliminates the challenges mentioned above [8]. The fast pyrolysis process also produces char as a by-product but in much lower quantities and with inferior characteristics compared to torrefaction processes [9]. Bio-coal is known as biochar and hydrochar for DT and WT, respectively. Compared to other chars produced in other processes, hydrochar has considerably better physicochemical properties, which allows it to be a potential replacement for fossil coal in power plants, and has all the advantages of coal [9]. Around 300 million tons of hard coal is used as solid fuel in Europe. Hence, the potential of bio-coal as a replacement for coal is significant [10]. The importance of using coal alternatives in jurisdictions like Alberta, which has a high dependency on fossil coal and is planning to phase out coal plants, is undeniable [11,12].

In an earlier study, the authors conducted detailed techno-economic assessments of the DT and WT of the feedstocks studied here (wheat straw, pine woodchips, grape pomace, manure and algae) [11]. How- ever, the energy and emissions performances of torrefaction processes need to be evaluated given the lack of studies in this area. While there are several life cycle assessment (LCA) studies of various biomass con- version processes, such as pyrolysis and hydrothermal liquefaction [13-32], there are very few LCAs of electricity produced from bio-coal via DT and especially novel WT. WT is in the early development stage and many aspects of the process require more research [9]. Adams et al. conducted a cradle-to-gate LCA of the DT of wood using ReCiPe and compared the results with pelletization [33]. Their results showed that torrefaction offers more benefits than pelletization (the fuel has a higher calorific value, and the climate change and fossil depletion impacts are lower than pelletization's). Key limitations of their study are that the materials required for planting, growth, and cultivating equipment were not included in the evaluation and the reactor drying energy require- ment was assumed to be 3 MJ/kg water removed. McNamee et al. investigated life cycle GHG emissions of DT of North American pine and found that DT generates fewer emissions than pelletization [8]. A key limitation of this study is that the required fertilizers, pesticides, and soil carbon, and land-use change emissions were not included in the evalu- ation. Earlier studies to evaluate the environmental performance of WT have been limited to two recent works by Owsianiak et al. and Berge et al. [10,34]. Berge et al. conducted an LCA of the energy production from the hydrochar of food waste [34]. The key limitations of their study include assuming the incineration of municipal solid waste for food waste hydrochar combustion and omitting the following in the system boundary and hence the evaluation: feedstock collection, electricity consumption for feed pumping into the reactor, and hydrochar drying [34]. Owsianiak et al. used ecoinvent and SimaPro to study the envi- ronmental performance of the production of heat (not electricity) from the firing of hydrochar pellets produced from four types of biomass (garden trimmings, orange peels, the organic fraction of municipal solid waste, and digestate), all with high moisture content, in Europe and for European conditions with data specific to Spain or Germany [10]. However, the moisture content of the feedstock and the hydrochar composition have a great effect on environmental performance, and the overall environmental evaluation of torrefaction processes is signifi- cantly affected by geographical location, the waste management ap- proaches already in place, the electricity mix in a specific area, and the type of fuel being substituted [10,34]. Moreover, the collection and transportation stages of feedstocks have been omitted in many LCA studies of waste management systems, which is another gap in the body of knowledge [10,35,36]. Hence, to the best of the authors' knowledge, there is very limited work on the LCA of torrefaction processes and there are no comparative analyses of a wide range of feedstocks (covering a wide range of moisture contents) for both torrefaction processes, as evaluated in this study. This study addresses the gaps found in earlier studies.
The key novelties of this research include:

<list>The estimation of net energy ratio (NER) and GHG emissions for the whole life cycle of electricity generation including biomass produc- tion, in-field preparation, biomass transportation, the torrefaction process, bio-coal transportation, and electricity generation in the power plant (from bio-coals produced via DT and WT processes); 
A comparative evaluation of five different types of biomass covering a wide range of feedstock moisture contents (15-90%) from various sectors including agricultural, municipal, and forestry; 
A comparative assessment of WT and DT processes for the production of bio-coals used for electricity generation from various biomasses and both technologies.</list>

The overall objective of this study is to conduct comparative as- sessments of energy and GHG emissions over the life cycle of electricity generation from biomass through to bio-coal and to compare the GHG reduction potential of bio-coal-based electricity generation to fossil- based ones as well as the energy consumption of electricity production from various biomass feedstocks converted to bio-coals. The present study involves: development of process models for each bio-coal pro- duction pathway; estimation of the net energy ratio for wet and dry torrefaction process-based bio-coal over the life cycle of electricity generation; estimation of the GHG emissions for wet and dry torre- faction process-based bio-coal over the life cycle of electricity genera- tion; a comparative analysis of wet and dry torrefaction processes of five different types of biomass; and, an analysis of sensitivity for all the different pathways to understand the effects of variation in different input parameters on the life cycle GHG emissions and net energy ratio.

However, the moisture content of the feedstock and the hydrochar composition have a great effect on environmental performance, and the overall environmental evaluation of torrefaction processes is signifi- cantly affected by geographical location, the waste management ap- proaches already in place, the electricity mix in a specific area, and the type of fuel being substituted [10,34]. Moreover, the collection and transportation stages of feedstocks have been omitted in many LCA studies of waste management systems, which is another gap in the body of knowledge [10,35,36]. Hence, to the best of the authors' knowledge, there is very limited work on the LCA of torrefaction processes and there are no comparative analyses of a wide range of feedstocks (covering a wide range of moisture contents) for both torrefaction processes, as evaluated in this study. This study addresses the gaps found in earlier studies.
The key novelties of this research include:

<list>The estimation of net energy ratio (NER) and GHG emissions for the whole life cycle of electricity generation including biomass produc- tion, in-field preparation, biomass transportation, the torrefaction process, bio-coal transportation, and electricity generation in the power plant (from bio-coals produced via DT and WT processes); 
A comparative evaluation of five different types of biomass covering a wide range of feedstock moisture contents (15-90%) from various sectors including agricultural, municipal, and forestry; 
A comparative assessment of WT and DT processes for the production of bio-coals used for electricity generation from various biomasses and both technologies.</list>

The overall objective of this study is to conduct comparative as- sessments of energy and GHG emissions over the life cycle of electricity generation from biomass through to bio-coal and to compare the GHG reduction potential of bio-coal-based electricity generation to fossil- based ones as well as the energy consumption of electricity production from various biomass feedstocks converted to bio-coals. The present study involves: development of process models for each bio-coal pro- duction pathway; estimation of the net energy ratio for wet and dry torrefaction process-based bio-coal over the life cycle of electricity generation; estimation of the GHG emissions for wet and dry torre- faction process-based bio-coal over the life cycle of electricity genera- tion; a comparative analysis of wet and dry torrefaction processes of five different types of biomass; and, an analysis of sensitivity for all the different pathways to understand the effects of variation in different input parameters on the life cycle GHG emissions and net energy ratio.
7: Achieving a sustainable society requires a transition from fossil- based to renewable-based energy system. Global warming due to human activity is mainly a result of fossil fuel use. Global CO2 emissions due to human activities have increased by 2.2% per year between 2005 and 2015. To limit the global average temperature increase to less than 2% (a goal of the Paris Agreement), CO2 emissions needs to be reduced [1].

Renewable energy use has grown significantly in the power gener- ation sector [2]. By 2040, its electricity generation share will triple because of new policies and make up more than 40% of overall elec- tricity generation [2]. There are some concerns regarding the sustain- ability of biofuels when feedstock can also be a source of food (i.e., biofuels can not only compete with food production for agricultural land

but their production can also lead to land clearing and cultivation, which increase in CO2 emissions) [2]. Currently, biomass residues/by-products are treated as waste and are underused. A bio-based economy can be a pillar of a sustainable society wherein biomass residues/wastes or organic wastes are sources of clean energy and products such as high-value bio-based chemicals, fuels, and elec- tricity [3].

The global share of bioenergy in the primary energy mix is 13%, more than any other renewable source [4]. However, biomass on its own has some disadvantages such as low mass and energy densities, low heating value, and non-homogeneity; poor grindability, hydrophilicity, seasonality and storage stability; and high volatile, oxygen, and mois- ture content, all of which make its handling, transportation, and use difficult and costly [5-7].
Dry torrefaction (DT) and novel wet torrefaction (WT) processes are

two possible ways of converting residual biomass feedstocks to bio-coal that eliminates the challenges mentioned above [8]. The fast pyrolysis process also produces char as a by-product but in much lower quantities and with inferior characteristics compared to torrefaction processes [9]. Bio-coal is known as biochar and hydrochar for DT and WT, respectively. Compared to other chars produced in other processes, hydrochar has considerably better physicochemical properties, which allows it to be a potential replacement for fossil coal in power plants, and has all the advantages of coal [9]. Around 300 million tons of hard coal is used as solid fuel in Europe. Hence, the potential of bio-coal as a replacement for coal is significant [10]. The importance of using coal alternatives in jurisdictions like Alberta, which has a high dependency on fossil coal and is planning to phase out coal plants, is undeniable [11,12].

In an earlier study, the authors conducted detailed techno-economic assessments of the DT and WT of the feedstocks studied here (wheat straw, pine woodchips, grape pomace, manure and algae) [11]. How- ever, the energy and emissions performances of torrefaction processes need to be evaluated given the lack of studies in this area. While there are several life cycle assessment (LCA) studies of various biomass con- version processes, such as pyrolysis and hydrothermal liquefaction [13-32], there are very few LCAs of electricity produced from bio-coal via DT and especially novel WT. WT is in the early development stage and many aspects of the process require more research [9]. Adams et al. conducted a cradle-to-gate LCA of the DT of wood using ReCiPe and compared the results with pelletization [33]. Their results showed that torrefaction offers more benefits than pelletization (the fuel has a higher calorific value, and the climate change and fossil depletion impacts are lower than pelletization's). Key limitations of their study are that the materials required for planting, growth, and cultivating equipment were not included in the evaluation and the reactor drying energy require- ment was assumed to be 3 MJ/kg water removed. McNamee et al. investigated life cycle GHG emissions of DT of North American pine and found that DT generates fewer emissions than pelletization [8]. A key limitation of this study is that the required fertilizers, pesticides, and soil carbon, and land-use change emissions were not included in the evalu- ation. Earlier studies to evaluate the environmental performance of WT have been limited to two recent works by Owsianiak et al. and Berge et al. [10,34]. Berge et al. conducted an LCA of the energy production from the hydrochar of food waste [34]. The key limitations of their study include assuming the incineration of municipal solid waste for food waste hydrochar combustion and omitting the following in the system boundary and hence the evaluation: feedstock collection, electricity consumption for feed pumping into the reactor, and hydrochar drying [34]. Owsianiak et al. used ecoinvent and SimaPro to study the envi- ronmental performance of the production of heat (not electricity) from the firing of hydrochar pellets produced from four types of biomass (garden trimmings, orange peels, the organic fraction of municipal solid waste, and digestate), all with high moisture content, in Europe and for European conditions with data specific to Spain or Germany [10]. However, the moisture content of the feedstock and the hydrochar composition have a great effect on environmental performance, and the overall environmental evaluation of torrefaction processes is signifi- cantly affected by geographical location, the waste management ap- proaches already in place, the electricity mix in a specific area, and the type of fuel being substituted [10,34]. Moreover, the collection and transportation stages of feedstocks have been omitted in many LCA studies of waste management systems, which is another gap in the body of knowledge [10,35,36]. Hence, to the best of the authors' knowledge, there is very limited work on the LCA of torrefaction processes and there are no comparative analyses of a wide range of feedstocks (covering a wide range of moisture contents) for both torrefaction processes, as evaluated in this study. This study addresses the gaps found in earlier studies.
The key novelties of this research include:

<list>The estimation of net energy ratio (NER) and GHG emissions for the whole life cycle of electricity generation including biomass produc- tion, in-field preparation, biomass transportation, the torrefaction process, bio-coal transportation, and electricity generation in the power plant (from bio-coals produced via DT and WT processes); 
A comparative evaluation of five different types of biomass covering a wide range of feedstock moisture contents (15-90%) from various sectors including agricultural, municipal, and forestry; 
A comparative assessment of WT and DT processes for the production of bio-coals used for electricity generation from various biomasses and both technologies.</list>

The overall objective of this study is to conduct comparative as- sessments of energy and GHG emissions over the life cycle of electricity generation from biomass through to bio-coal and to compare the GHG reduction potential of bio-coal-based electricity generation to fossil- based ones as well as the energy consumption of electricity production from various biomass feedstocks converted to bio-coals. The present study involves: development of process models for each bio-coal pro- duction pathway; estimation of the net energy ratio for wet and dry torrefaction process-based bio-coal over the life cycle of electricity generation; estimation of the GHG emissions for wet and dry torre- faction process-based bio-coal over the life cycle of electricity genera- tion; a comparative analysis of wet and dry torrefaction processes of five different types of biomass; and, an analysis of sensitivity for all the different pathways to understand the effects of variation in different input parameters on the life cycle GHG emissions and net energy ratio.

9: LCA starts with defining the inventory, which comprises all energy and material inputs and outputs, followed by assessing their associated environmental impacts. The assessed impacts are interpreted by con- verting them to impact categories like global warming potential, rep- resented by a CO2 equivalent (CO2-e) indicator using Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) factors [37].

The main goal of this study is to analyze and evaluate life cycle NER and GHG emissions of electricity generation using bio-coal produced from various biomass feedstocks. The results have been compared for 10 pathways (2 torrefaction processes and 5 feedstocks) based on energy consumption and GHG production over the different life cycle stages. The comparative life cycle assessment helps choose which combination (feedstock and process) leads to the highest energy return and lowest GHG emissions. The following sections discuss in detail system bound- aries, functional unit, assumptions, and limitations.
10: The torrefaction plant is assumed to be located in Alberta, Canada and hence, all the extracted data is specific to Alberta.

It was assumed in this study that there is no mass loss during elec- tricity production from biomass via DT and WT. In reality, this assumption is not achievable because of biomass losses in the trans- portation, storage, and processing stages [10,33].

Some stages have been shown to have a very low impact on overall life cycle GHG emissions and energy consumption of biochar production (via pyrolysis), i.e., farm equipment manufacturing, plant construction and dismantling [20,38]. Plant infrastructure and construction emis- sions of pyrolysis and hydrothermal liquefaction plants were found to make a very small share of total emissions (less than 1% for both pro- cesses) [20,38] and many studies of thermochemical conversion of biomass do not include these emissions [20,39,40]. While environ- mental impacts such as mineral extraction and ecotoxicity are high for plant construction, decommissioning, and farm equipment

manufacturing, they typically are responsible for less than 5% of the total GHG emissions of pellet combustion [41,42]. In the case of torre- faction, plant and storage infrastructure made negligible impacts compared to other stages of the process [33]. In this study, plant con- struction and dismantling, as well as farm equipment, are not included in the GHG emissions calculations. Energy consumption from farm equipment manufacturing and plant construction and dismantling are also left out as they have minimal contributions [38,43]. Plant con- struction, for example, accounts for only 2-4% of the total energy con- sumption for biochar production via pyrolysis [38].

12: Two approaches can be used to evaluate GHG emissions: an evalu- ation of the life cycle GHG emissions' reduction from bio-coal compared to coal and an evaluation of only the stable carbon in the bio-coal [38]. In this study, the former approach is taken because it leads to a comprehensive outlook. This approach adds more environmental value to bio-coal as large amounts of GHGs are emitted during the extraction and transportation of coal from the mine to the power plant. CO2 emissions from the combustion of bio-coal in a power plant are assumed to be zero because the CO2 produced is biogenic, originating from bio-based raw materials (bio-coal) and absorbed during plant growth [44]. The LCA conducted in this study covers energy and GHG emissions from the cultivation or extraction of raw materials to their final use or disposal.
Fig. 1 illustrates the LCA system boundaries of electricity generation from bio-coal produced via wet and dry torrefaction processes.

Five feedstocks with different moisture contents are studied here. Table 1 lists the moisture content of the feedstocks and the temperature and residence time of the torrefaction processes. Table 1 also shows the characteristics of the associated bio-coal produced through each process and from each feedstock. Comparing the bio-coal ultimate analysis with coal shows that bio-coal can be used as a replacement for different ranks of coal depending on the bio-coal characteristics [45].

14: 2.3.1.1. Pine woodchips, grape pomace, and manure. Pine woodchips, grape pomace, and manure are considered waste/residue because they are produced automatically during production of a primary product (in this case, logs, grape juice, and livestock maintenance) and have zero or limited value [11,65-72]. According to ISO 14040 and the GHG proto- col, if by-products have some value, emissions allocation is used [73]. There are several ways to allocate emissions to the multiple co-products of the same process including allocation based on mass, energy, and economic value [69,70]. Each method has its own advantages and dis- advantages and SO the results can differ significantly [69,74,75]. For example, allocation based on economic value leads to the addition of dealing with the price fluctuation's complexity over time and external influences (such as the effect of political decisions on emissions assess- ment) [69]. Emissions allocation brings uncertainty to the results because of the significant influence of each allocation method on the results [74]. To avoid this issue, the ISO LCA method recommends avoiding emissions allocation and expanding the boundaries instead (hence taking into account the additional functions of by-products) [71, 74-76], which is out of the scope of this study. However, both system boundary expansion and emissions allocation lead to limitations in comparing the results of different studies because of the differences in allocation methods (subjective underlying assumption) [74].

As assumed in several studies [65-72,77], the by-products, including various types of wastes and agricultural crop residues such as manure, straw, and wood residues, have low or zero economic value and hence the emissions associated with the process are exclusively allocated to the main product. For example, emissions released during the cultivation of wheat (the main product of the cultivating process) is allocated to the main product, grain, not straw [70].

Surplus wood residues such as sawdust, wood shavings, bark, and sludge are landfilled in privately owned and forest industry-operated facilities and the residues are treated as waste [76,78]. Although there have been some efforts to use grape pomace, most of it is disposed of in landfills [79,80].

Fig. 1. System boundary for electricity production from torrefied biomasses: a) wheat straw, b) pine woodchips, c) algae. Grape pomace and manure system boundaries start from the torrefaction process. (TI: first transportation stage, TII: second transportation satge)

Hence, no GHG emissions and energy associated with the production of pine woodchips, grape pomace, or manure were considered.

2.3.1.2. Wheat straw. Wheat straw is a residue/by-product from wheat production. As explained above, wheat grain as the primary product is responsible for all environmental impacts and fossil energy use of wheat grain production [70,77]. Hence, none of the environmental and energy costs associated with farming operations such as cultivation and fertil- ization are allocated to wheat straw.

However, removing wheat straw from the field has an impact; it affects the carbon content of the soil and leads to the loss of nitrogen and other soil nutrients. In order to maintain soil nutrients, fertilizer needs to be added. The energy and GHG emissions from the production and application of an equivalent amount of fertilizer to the soil are taken into account in this study [81]. The impact of straw removal on soil carbon is hard to determine [8], SO it is not included in this study. Over a 50-year perspective, 30% of the total N in straw is available to crops as fertilizer N [81]. Table 2 lists the extra fertilizer required. 49.45 MJ is required per kg of N and the associated GHG emissions are 3.58 g CO2-e/g N [82].

To investigate the effect of different emissions allocation methods on emissions released during the production stage, two case studies for electricity generation from wheat straw biochar and hydrochar were conducted, and the results are presented in Section 3.3 (Sensitivity analysis). The emissions generated during cultivation and harvesting such as farm machinery operations as well as required farming fertilizer and pesticide were considered using the emission factors reported by Yan et al. [83]. Annual application rates were calculated based on the area required to harvest the input wheat straw to torrefaction plant and using a wheat straw yield of 0.63 dt/ha [84].

Three emissions allocation methods - allocation based on mass, en- ergy, and market value - were used to allocate the emissions from production to both wheat grain and wheat straw. The allocation factor was calculated using the following equation [85,86]:
Emissions allocation factor = Total emissions X Mass/energy/economic value of straw produced per year Total mass/energy/economic value of both wheat straw and wheat grain per year

2.3.1.3. Algae. Different methods are used for algae cultivation, drying, lipid extraction, and separation, leading to significantly large differences in energy consumption and GHG emissions' results [18,87,88]. In this study, Canada-specific algae production data developed by Pankratz
Table 2 Extra fertilizer required due to the removal of straw from fields.

et al. was used [89,90]. According to Pankratz et al., currently algae is cultivated predominantly in open pond raceway (OPR) and photo- bioreactor (PBR) systems. OPR systems are generally found in warmer climates, which provide favorable growing temperatures. PBR systems are generally enclosed; this allows for closely controlled environmental conditions and therefore produces more consistent and higher yields. However, these advantages are offset in PBRs by higher capital and operating costs, which similarly increase associated environmental im- pacts. GHG emissions from the production of 1 tonne of algae are -0.9 tonne of CO2-e, and the amount of CO2-e absorbed during 1 tonne of algae growth is 1.8 tonne; hence the CO2 absorbed by the algae is twice that of dry algae [90,91]. Therefore, the net CO2-e emitted during algae production is 0.8 tonne per tonne of algae, and the energy required is 12.7 MJ/kg algae.

2.3.2.1. Pine woodchips. The GHG emissions in this stage are from the consumption of diesel during loading and chipping the residues [92]. Loading and chipping 1 bone dry metric tonne of residues require 3.83 L diesel [92] and 82.3 MJ of energy per tonne of residues [82].

2.3.2.2. Wheat straw. Processing operations such as raking, pressing (baling), wrapping, stacking, and handling in the field are considered. Primary energy requirements and the associated GHG emissions were calculated to be 1067.10 MJ/t wheat straw and 145.43 kg/t wheat straw using the data reported by Elsayed et al. [93].
2.3.2.3. Algae. The energy and environmental impacts of algae in field preparation can be categorized under the whole production process of algae.
15: 2.3.1.1. Pine woodchips, grape pomace, and manure. To avoid this issue, the ISO LCA method recommends avoiding emissions allocation and expanding the boundaries instead (hence taking into account the additional functions of by-products) [71, 74-76], which is out of the scope of this study. However, both system boundary expansion and emissions allocation lead to limitations in comparing the results of different studies because of the differences in allocation methods (subjective underlying assumption) [74].

As assumed in several studies [65-72,77], the by-products, including various types of wastes and agricultural crop residues such as manure, straw, and wood residues, have low or zero economic value and hence the emissions associated with the process are exclusively allocated to the main product. For example, emissions released during the cultivation of wheat (the main product of the cultivating process) is allocated to the main product, grain, not straw [70].

Surplus wood residues such as sawdust, wood shavings, bark, and sludge are landfilled in privately owned and forest industry-operated facilities and the residues are treated as waste [76,78]. Although there have been some efforts to use grape pomace, most of it is disposed of in landfills [79,80].

2.3.1.2. Wheat straw. Wheat straw is a residue/by-product from wheat production. As explained above, wheat grain as the primary product is responsible for all environmental impacts and fossil energy use of wheat grain production [70,77]. Hence, none of the environmental and energy costs associated with farming operations such as cultivation and fertil- ization are allocated to wheat straw.

However, removing wheat straw from the field has an impact; it affects the carbon content of the soil and leads to the loss of nitrogen and other soil nutrients. In order to maintain soil nutrients, fertilizer needs to be added. The energy and GHG emissions from the production and application of an equivalent amount of fertilizer to the soil are taken into account in this study [81]. The impact of straw removal on soil carbon is hard to determine [8], SO it is not included in this study. Over a 50-year perspective, 30% of the total N in straw is available to crops as fertilizer N [81]. Table 2 lists the extra fertilizer required. 49.45 MJ is required per kg of N and the associated GHG emissions are 3.58 g CO2-e/g N [82].

To investigate the effect of different emissions allocation methods on emissions released during the production stage, two case studies for electricity generation from wheat straw biochar and hydrochar were conducted, and the results are presented in Section 3.3 (Sensitivity analysis). The emissions generated during cultivation and harvesting such as farm machinery operations as well as required farming fertilizer and pesticide were considered using the emission factors reported by Yan et al. [83]. Annual application rates were calculated based on the area required to harvest the input wheat straw to torrefaction plant and using a wheat straw yield of 0.63 dt/ha [84].

Three emissions allocation methods - allocation based on mass, en- ergy, and market value - were used to allocate the emissions from production to both wheat grain and wheat straw. The allocation factor was calculated using the following equation [85,86]:
Emissions allocation factor = Total emissions X Mass/energy/economic value of straw produced per year Total mass/energy/economic value of both wheat straw and wheat grain per year

2.3.1.3. Algae. Different methods are used for algae cultivation, drying, lipid extraction, and separation, leading to significantly large differences in energy consumption and GHG emissions' results [18,87,88]. In this study, Canada-specific algae production data developed by Pankratz
Table 2 Extra fertilizer required due to the removal of straw from fields.

et al. was used [89,90]. According to Pankratz et al., currently algae is cultivated predominantly in open pond raceway (OPR) and photo- bioreactor (PBR) systems. OPR systems are generally found in warmer climates, which provide favorable growing temperatures. PBR systems are generally enclosed; this allows for closely controlled environmental conditions and therefore produces more consistent and higher yields. However, these advantages are offset in PBRs by higher capital and operating costs, which similarly increase associated environmental im- pacts. GHG emissions from the production of 1 tonne of algae are -0.9 tonne of CO2-e, and the amount of CO2-e absorbed during 1 tonne of algae growth is 1.8 tonne; hence the CO2 absorbed by the algae is twice that of dry algae [90,91]. Therefore, the net CO2-e emitted during algae production is 0.8 tonne per tonne of algae, and the energy required is 12.7 MJ/kg algae.

2.3.2.1. Pine woodchips. The GHG emissions in this stage are from the consumption of diesel during loading and chipping the residues [92]. Loading and chipping 1 bone dry metric tonne of residues require 3.83 L diesel [92] and 82.3 MJ of energy per tonne of residues [82].

2.3.2.2. Wheat straw. Processing operations such as raking, pressing (baling), wrapping, stacking, and handling in the field are considered. Primary energy requirements and the associated GHG emissions were calculated to be 1067.10 MJ/t wheat straw and 145.43 kg/t wheat straw using the data reported by Elsayed et al. [93].
2.3.2.3. Algae. The energy and environmental impacts of algae in field preparation can be categorized under the whole production process of algae.
16: 2.3.1.1. Pine woodchips, grape pomace, and manure. Pine woodchips, grape pomace, and manure are considered waste/residue because they are produced automatically during production of a primary product (in this case, logs, grape juice, and livestock maintenance) and have zero or limited value [11,65-72]. According to ISO 14040 and the GHG proto- col, if by-products have some value, emissions allocation is used [73]. There are several ways to allocate emissions to the multiple co-products of the same process including allocation based on mass, energy, and economic value [69,70]. Each method has its own advantages and dis- advantages and SO the results can differ significantly [69,74,75]. For example, allocation based on economic value leads to the addition of dealing with the price fluctuation's complexity over time and external influences (such as the effect of political decisions on emissions assess- ment) [69]. Emissions allocation brings uncertainty to the results because of the significant influence of each allocation method on the results [74]. To avoid this issue, the ISO LCA method recommends avoiding emissions allocation and expanding the boundaries instead (hence taking into account the additional functions of by-products) [71, 74-76], which is out of the scope of this study. However, both system boundary expansion and emissions allocation lead to limitations in comparing the results of different studies because of the differences in allocation methods (subjective underlying assumption) [74].

Each method has its own advantages and dis- advantages and SO the results can differ significantly [69,74,75]. For example, allocation based on economic value leads to the addition of dealing with the price fluctuation's complexity over time and external influences (such as the effect of political decisions on emissions assess- ment) [69]. Emissions allocation brings uncertainty to the results because of the significant influence of each allocation method on the results [74]. To avoid this issue, the ISO LCA method recommends avoiding emissions allocation and expanding the boundaries instead (hence taking into account the additional functions of by-products) [71, 74-76], which is out of the scope of this study. However, both system boundary expansion and emissions allocation lead to limitations in comparing the results of different studies because of the differences in allocation methods (subjective underlying assumption) [74].

As assumed in several studies [65-72,77], the by-products, including various types of wastes and agricultural crop residues such as manure, straw, and wood residues, have low or zero economic value and hence the emissions associated with the process are exclusively allocated to the main product. For example, emissions released during the cultivation of wheat (the main product of the cultivating process) is allocated to the main product, grain, not straw [70].

Surplus wood residues such as sawdust, wood shavings, bark, and sludge are landfilled in privately owned and forest industry-operated facilities and the residues are treated as waste [76,78]. Although there have been some efforts to use grape pomace, most of it is disposed of in landfills [79,80].

2.3.1.2. Wheat straw. Wheat straw is a residue/by-product from wheat production. As explained above, wheat grain as the primary product is responsible for all environmental impacts and fossil energy use of wheat grain production [70,77]. Hence, none of the environmental and energy costs associated with farming operations such as cultivation and fertil- ization are allocated to wheat straw.

However, removing wheat straw from the field has an impact; it affects the carbon content of the soil and leads to the loss of nitrogen and other soil nutrients. In order to maintain soil nutrients, fertilizer needs to be added. The energy and GHG emissions from the production and application of an equivalent amount of fertilizer to the soil are taken into account in this study [81]. The impact of straw removal on soil carbon is hard to determine [8], SO it is not included in this study. Over a 50-year perspective, 30% of the total N in straw is available to crops as fertilizer N [81]. Table 2 lists the extra fertilizer required. 49.45 MJ is required per kg of N and the associated GHG emissions are 3.58 g CO2-e/g N [82].

To investigate the effect of different emissions allocation methods on emissions released during the production stage, two case studies for electricity generation from wheat straw biochar and hydrochar were conducted, and the results are presented in Section 3.3 (Sensitivity analysis). The emissions generated during cultivation and harvesting such as farm machinery operations as well as required farming fertilizer and pesticide were considered using the emission factors reported by Yan et al. [83]. Annual application rates were calculated based on the area required to harvest the input wheat straw to torrefaction plant and using a wheat straw yield of 0.63 dt/ha [84].

Three emissions allocation methods - allocation based on mass, en- ergy, and market value - were used to allocate the emissions from production to both wheat grain and wheat straw. The allocation factor was calculated using the following equation [85,86]:
Emissions allocation factor = Total emissions X Mass/energy/economic value of straw produced per year Total mass/energy/economic value of both wheat straw and wheat grain per year

2.3.1.3. Algae. Different methods are used for algae cultivation, drying, lipid extraction, and separation, leading to significantly large differences in energy consumption and GHG emissions' results [18,87,88]. In this study, Canada-specific algae production data developed by Pankratz
Table 2 Extra fertilizer required due to the removal of straw from fields.

et al. was used [89,90]. According to Pankratz et al., currently algae is cultivated predominantly in open pond raceway (OPR) and photo- bioreactor (PBR) systems. OPR systems are generally found in warmer climates, which provide favorable growing temperatures. PBR systems are generally enclosed; this allows for closely controlled environmental conditions and therefore produces more consistent and higher yields. However, these advantages are offset in PBRs by higher capital and operating costs, which similarly increase associated environmental im- pacts. GHG emissions from the production of 1 tonne of algae are -0.9 tonne of CO2-e, and the amount of CO2-e absorbed during 1 tonne of algae growth is 1.8 tonne; hence the CO2 absorbed by the algae is twice that of dry algae [90,91]. Therefore, the net CO2-e emitted during algae production is 0.8 tonne per tonne of algae, and the energy required is 12.7 MJ/kg algae.

2.3.2.1. Pine woodchips. The GHG emissions in this stage are from the consumption of diesel during loading and chipping the residues [92]. Loading and chipping 1 bone dry metric tonne of residues require 3.83 L diesel [92] and 82.3 MJ of energy per tonne of residues [82].

2.3.2.2. Wheat straw. Processing operations such as raking, pressing (baling), wrapping, stacking, and handling in the field are considered. Primary energy requirements and the associated GHG emissions were calculated to be 1067.10 MJ/t wheat straw and 145.43 kg/t wheat straw using the data reported by Elsayed et al. [93].
2.3.2.3. Algae. The energy and environmental impacts of algae in field preparation can be categorized under the whole production process of algae.

2.3.2.3. Algae. The energy and environmental impacts of algae in field preparation can be categorized under the whole production process of algae.
23: 2.3.1.1. Pine woodchips, grape pomace, and manure. Pine woodchips, grape pomace, and manure are considered waste/residue because they are produced automatically during production of a primary product (in this case, logs, grape juice, and livestock maintenance) and have zero or limited value [11,65-72]. According to ISO 14040 and the GHG proto- col, if by-products have some value, emissions allocation is used [73]. There are several ways to allocate emissions to the multiple co-products of the same process including allocation based on mass, energy, and economic value [69,70]. Each method has its own advantages and dis- advantages and SO the results can differ significantly [69,74,75]. For example, allocation based on economic value leads to the addition of dealing with the price fluctuation's complexity over time and external influences (such as the effect of political decisions on emissions assess- ment) [69]. Emissions allocation brings uncertainty to the results because of the significant influence of each allocation method on the results [74]. To avoid this issue, the ISO LCA method recommends avoiding emissions allocation and expanding the boundaries instead (hence taking into account the additional functions of by-products) [71, 74-76], which is out of the scope of this study. However, both system boundary expansion and emissions allocation lead to limitations in comparing the results of different studies because of the differences in allocation methods (subjective underlying assumption) [74].

As assumed in several studies [65-72,77], the by-products, including various types of wastes and agricultural crop residues such as manure, straw, and wood residues, have low or zero economic value and hence the emissions associated with the process are exclusively allocated to the main product. For example, emissions released during the cultivation of wheat (the main product of the cultivating process) is allocated to the main product, grain, not straw [70].

Surplus wood residues such as sawdust, wood shavings, bark, and sludge are landfilled in privately owned and forest industry-operated facilities and the residues are treated as waste [76,78]. Although there have been some efforts to use grape pomace, most of it is disposed of in landfills [79,80].

Hence, no GHG emissions and energy associated with the production of pine woodchips, grape pomace, or manure were considered.

2.3.1.2. Wheat straw. Wheat straw is a residue/by-product from wheat production. As explained above, wheat grain as the primary product is responsible for all environmental impacts and fossil energy use of wheat grain production [70,77]. Hence, none of the environmental and energy costs associated with farming operations such as cultivation and fertil- ization are allocated to wheat straw.

However, removing wheat straw from the field has an impact; it affects the carbon content of the soil and leads to the loss of nitrogen and other soil nutrients. In order to maintain soil nutrients, fertilizer needs to be added. The energy and GHG emissions from the production and application of an equivalent amount of fertilizer to the soil are taken into account in this study [81]. The impact of straw removal on soil carbon is hard to determine [8], SO it is not included in this study. Over a 50-year perspective, 30% of the total N in straw is available to crops as fertilizer N [81]. Table 2 lists the extra fertilizer required. 49.45 MJ is required per kg of N and the associated GHG emissions are 3.58 g CO2-e/g N [82].

To investigate the effect of different emissions allocation methods on emissions released during the production stage, two case studies for electricity generation from wheat straw biochar and hydrochar were conducted, and the results are presented in Section 3.3 (Sensitivity analysis). The emissions generated during cultivation and harvesting such as farm machinery operations as well as required farming fertilizer and pesticide were considered using the emission factors reported by Yan et al. [83]. Annual application rates were calculated based on the area required to harvest the input wheat straw to torrefaction plant and using a wheat straw yield of 0.63 dt/ha [84].

Three emissions allocation methods - allocation based on mass, en- ergy, and market value - were used to allocate the emissions from production to both wheat grain and wheat straw. The allocation factor was calculated using the following equation [85,86]:
Emissions allocation factor = Total emissions X Mass/energy/economic value of straw produced per year Total mass/energy/economic value of both wheat straw and wheat grain per year

2.3.1.3. Algae. Different methods are used for algae cultivation, drying, lipid extraction, and separation, leading to significantly large differences in energy consumption and GHG emissions' results [18,87,88]. In this study, Canada-specific algae production data developed by Pankratz
Table 2 Extra fertilizer required due to the removal of straw from fields.

et al. was used [89,90]. According to Pankratz et al., currently algae is cultivated predominantly in open pond raceway (OPR) and photo- bioreactor (PBR) systems. OPR systems are generally found in warmer climates, which provide favorable growing temperatures. PBR systems are generally enclosed; this allows for closely controlled environmental conditions and therefore produces more consistent and higher yields. However, these advantages are offset in PBRs by higher capital and operating costs, which similarly increase associated environmental im- pacts. GHG emissions from the production of 1 tonne of algae are -0.9 tonne of CO2-e, and the amount of CO2-e absorbed during 1 tonne of algae growth is 1.8 tonne; hence the CO2 absorbed by the algae is twice that of dry algae [90,91]. Therefore, the net CO2-e emitted during algae production is 0.8 tonne per tonne of algae, and the energy required is 12.7 MJ/kg algae.

2.3.2.1. Pine woodchips. The GHG emissions in this stage are from the consumption of diesel during loading and chipping the residues [92]. Loading and chipping 1 bone dry metric tonne of residues require 3.83 L diesel [92] and 82.3 MJ of energy per tonne of residues [82].

2.3.2.2. Wheat straw. Processing operations such as raking, pressing (baling), wrapping, stacking, and handling in the field are considered. Primary energy requirements and the associated GHG emissions were calculated to be 1067.10 MJ/t wheat straw and 145.43 kg/t wheat straw using the data reported by Elsayed et al. [93].
2.3.2.3. Algae. The energy and environmental impacts of algae in field preparation can be categorized under the whole production process of algae.
24: As mentioned above, we assumed that the torrefaction plant is located as close as possible to the production point and hence no storage was included. However, some biomass feedstocks such as wheat straw may require storage in the torrefaction plant. The duration of this required storage is linked to the feedstock availability throughout the year [94]. The storage of biomass can also generate emissions because of biomass degradation. These emissions are related to dry matter loss and are a function of storage environment, moisture content, and feedstock nature [8,94]. Higher temperatures lead to increased microbial activity and hence increased emissions [8]. Hence, biomass feedstocks should be used as fast as possible in the summer [8]. Biomass storage emissions have received limited research and attention and the resources are scarce for calculating emissions of biomass storage compared to that of other operations in biomass supply chains [94-96]. Many studies do not include storage emissions in their GHG assessment and assume that storage does not affect biomass properties, does not need energy, and does not generate GHG emissions [34,43,70,96,97]. Therefore, more
research to better understand GHG emissions from biomass feedstock storage is required [96].

The available research instead focuses on CO poisoning rather than GHG assessment during biomass storage [94]. There are inconsistent variations for biomass storage emissions reported in the literature [94, 96]. In addition, the results of some studies show that the GHG emissions generated through biomass storage are low and their impact on the supply chain is negligible (except when using a silo because storage losses are greatly influenced by storage infrastructure and moisture content) [94,97]. GREET does not include energy requirements and feedstock losses from biomass storage either [97]. Dry biomass such as wheat straw has additional challenges such as fragmentation losses during transportation and storage rather than GHG emissions, unlike wet biomass, which is subject to microbial degradation during storage [96]. "Best practices" in biomass storage may significantly decrease dry matter losses and hence GHG emissions during this stage [96]. However, further studies are required to optimize feedstock collection and storage.

Considering all the above-mentioned points and because Alberta has short summers, GHG emissions due to biomass storage were not taken into account in this study. Accordingly, it was assumed that the power plant size associated with each feedstock is a function of the amount of bio-coal produced, when a newly built power plant is required. When available coal power plants are used, since the input capacity of these plants is greater than the produced bio-coals, co-firing bio-coal is assumed to incorporate the bio-coals produced in the existing infrastructure.

It should be noted that since the input biomass to the torrefaction plant is assumed to be low (264 dt/day, corresponding to 88,000 dt/yr [11]] compared to the amounts generated in Alberta (as shown in Table 3) [98-100] and the amount assumed in several studies (2000 dt/day) for different biomass feedstock processing facilities in Alberta [89,90,101-106], we assumed that no special considerations are necessary for storing biomass and that there are no concerns related to a continuous supply of biomass. At a capacity of 264 dt/day, economies of scale are achieved for the first time, and the unit capital cost does not decrease at higher capacities for a torrefaction plant [12]. So, this ca- pacity is the smallest, most economical capacity.

2.3.3.1. Manure. In current practices in Alberta, some of the produced manure is stored for a time before it is applied to the fields. 60.2% of farms with livestock in Alberta store manure [99]. During manure storage, GHGs are released to the atmosphere as a result of bacterial activity and manure decomposition. Methane is emitted from livestock manure when manure decomposes anaerobically. According to the IPCC, the amount of anaerobic decomposition in manure depends on the management system and temperature (climate) [107]. The methane produced can be captured by putting lids or caps on lagoons or tanks where manure is stored; this can reduce the amount of methane escaping to atmosphere. The captured methane can be used as boiler, heater, or engine-generator fuel or it can be flared. The initrification-denitrification of nitrogen in manure can be responsible for N2O production during manure storage. Dry and aerobic management approaches increase N2O production because of the initial aerobic reaction required. The emis- sions released during the storage of manure were calculated and the

procedure is explained in the Supplementary information. However, these emissions were not included here based on the assumption that the manure is used immediately and that the torrefaction plant is located beside the field.

It is assumed that algae and grape pomace are continuously produced in the algae production field and food processing (or juice production) facility, respectively, and trans- ferred to the torrefaction plant, which is assumed to be beside the feedstock production facilities. Hence, no storage is required.

procedure is explained in the Supplementary information. However, these emissions were not included here based on the assumption that the manure is used immediately and that the torrefaction plant is located beside the field.

2.3.3.2. Grape pomace and algae. It is assumed that algae and grape pomace are continuously produced in the algae production field and food processing (or juice production) facility, respectively, and trans- ferred to the torrefaction plant, which is assumed to be beside the feedstock production facilities. Hence, no storage is required.
29: As mentioned above, we assumed that the torrefaction plant is located as close as possible to the production point and hence no storage was included. However, some biomass feedstocks such as wheat straw may require storage in the torrefaction plant. The duration of this required storage is linked to the feedstock availability throughout the year [94]. The storage of biomass can also generate emissions because of biomass degradation. These emissions are related to dry matter loss and are a function of storage environment, moisture content, and feedstock nature [8,94]. Higher temperatures lead to increased microbial activity and hence increased emissions [8]. Hence, biomass feedstocks should be used as fast as possible in the summer [8]. Biomass storage emissions have received limited research and attention and the resources are scarce for calculating emissions of biomass storage compared to that of other operations in biomass supply chains [94-96]. Many studies do not include storage emissions in their GHG assessment and assume that storage does not affect biomass properties, does not need energy, and does not generate GHG emissions [34,43,70,96,97]. Therefore, more
research to better understand GHG emissions from biomass feedstock storage is required [96].

The available research instead focuses on CO poisoning rather than GHG assessment during biomass storage [94]. There are inconsistent variations for biomass storage emissions reported in the literature [94, 96]. In addition, the results of some studies show that the GHG emissions generated through biomass storage are low and their impact on the supply chain is negligible (except when using a silo because storage losses are greatly influenced by storage infrastructure and moisture content) [94,97]. GREET does not include energy requirements and feedstock losses from biomass storage either [97]. Dry biomass such as wheat straw has additional challenges such as fragmentation losses during transportation and storage rather than GHG emissions, unlike wet biomass, which is subject to microbial degradation during storage [96]. "Best practices" in biomass storage may significantly decrease dry matter losses and hence GHG emissions during this stage [96]. However, further studies are required to optimize feedstock collection and storage.

Considering all the above-mentioned points and because Alberta has short summers, GHG emissions due to biomass storage were not taken into account in this study. Accordingly, it was assumed that the power plant size associated with each feedstock is a function of the amount of bio-coal produced, when a newly built power plant is required. When available coal power plants are used, since the input capacity of these plants is greater than the produced bio-coals, co-firing bio-coal is assumed to incorporate the bio-coals produced in the existing infrastructure.

It should be noted that since the input biomass to the torrefaction plant is assumed to be low (264 dt/day, corresponding to 88,000 dt/yr [11]] compared to the amounts generated in Alberta (as shown in Table 3) [98-100] and the amount assumed in several studies (2000 dt/day) for different biomass feedstock processing facilities in Alberta [89,90,101-106], we assumed that no special considerations are necessary for storing biomass and that there are no concerns related to a continuous supply of biomass. At a capacity of 264 dt/day, economies of scale are achieved for the first time, and the unit capital cost does not decrease at higher capacities for a torrefaction plant [12]. So, this ca- pacity is the smallest, most economical capacity.

2.3.3.1. Manure. In current practices in Alberta, some of the produced manure is stored for a time before it is applied to the fields. 60.2% of farms with livestock in Alberta store manure [99]. During manure storage, GHGs are released to the atmosphere as a result of bacterial activity and manure decomposition. Methane is emitted from livestock manure when manure decomposes anaerobically. According to the IPCC, the amount of anaerobic decomposition in manure depends on the management system and temperature (climate) [107]. The methane produced can be captured by putting lids or caps on lagoons or tanks where manure is stored; this can reduce the amount of methane escaping to atmosphere. The captured methane can be used as boiler, heater, or engine-generator fuel or it can be flared. The initrification-denitrification of nitrogen in manure can be responsible for N2O production during manure storage. Dry and aerobic management approaches increase N2O production because of the initial aerobic reaction required. The emis- sions released during the storage of manure were calculated and the

2.3.3.2. Grape pomace and algae. It is assumed that algae and grape pomace are continuously produced in the algae production field and food processing (or juice production) facility, respectively, and trans- ferred to the torrefaction plant, which is assumed to be beside the feedstock production facilities. Hence, no storage is required.
30: In this study, we assumed that the torrefaction plants are located between feedstock production/collection points and power plants to minimize the feedstock transportation distance rather than the bio-coal transportation distance. The feedstocks studied here are characterized by their nonhomogeneity, voluminosity, and high moisture content; these require a greater transportation effort than bio-coals, which are relatively dry, homogenous, and densified (due to the loss of volatiles during torrefaction) and impose less energy and environmental impacts.

Transportation involves loading, unloading, and trucking. Two transportation stages were considered: from the production/collection points to the torrefaction plants (for feedstock) and from the torrefaction plants to the power plant (for bio-coals). Heavy duty trucks and diesel were considered to transport the feedstocks to the torrefaction plants [108]. According to Natural Resources Canada data, the energy intensity or fuel efficiency of diesel in heavy duty trucks in Alberta is 1.41 MJ/t km [109]. It should be noted that a plant's geographical location plays a key role in transport energy intensities and transport efficiencies; the transport energy intensities of European countries are between 0.81 and 0.87 MJ/t km for a 40 tonne diesel truck [77]. The energy factor or non-renewable (non-ren) energy input to energy output for diesel is 1.2 MJ/MJ diesel [110,111]. This number can also be derived by dividing the energy density and the inherent energy of fuel. The energy density of diesel in Canada is 45.25 MJ non-ren/L diesel and its inherent energy is 38.64 MJ/L diesel [82,112]. In other geographical locations such as Australia, however, diesel energy density has been reported at 47.76 MJ non-ren/L diesel [112]. The diesel emission factor (including upstream production and distribution as well as combustion) is 88.78 g CO2-e/MJ diesel [113].

2.3.4.1. Wheat straw. The total transportation distance for wheat straw was calculated based on the method explained in other studies [84,104, 106,114]. It was assumed that plant is located in the center of the field (center of a circular area) where wheat straw is harvested. The area of land required to produce the required wheat straw (input to the torre- faction plant) was calculated using the average wheat straw yield of 0.63 dt/ha [84]. Using the area of the land or production field (circle), the radius of the circle was calculated which is the maximum distance that wheat straw is transported to the torrefaction plant from the center of the circle (field). The average biomass transportation distance or the average distance is 2/3 of the maximum radius [84]. But round-trip transportation should be considered to take into account driving the empty truck from the torrefaction plant to the field and the full truck to the plant. A tortuosity factor of 1.27 was applied to the transportation distance to take into account special geographical conditions such as hills, swamps, and lakes in the field [84].
The total roundtrip transportation distance for wheat straw is 59.56 km and the input primary energy is 100.78 MJ/t of wheat straw.

2.3.4.2. Pine woodchips. For pine woodchips, a transportation distance of 55.52 km (determined following the same method as for wheat straw) from the field to the torrefaction plant and 93.90 MJ energy/t residue were considered.

2.3.4.3. Grape pomace, manure and algae. No transportation distances were considered for grape pomace, manure, or algae, on the assumption that the torrefaction plants were located close to the feedstock produc- tion/collection points. If loading and transport of grape pomace is considered to be comparable to digestate, 14 MJ energy/dt is required for pomace loading and transportation [115]. This does not change the overall NER of grape pomace to electricity (via torrefaction); hence, neglecting the energy and environment impacts of preparation and loading pomace is justifiable.

It is assumed that road transportation (trucks) is used for trans- porting bio-coals to power plants. However, we suggest that rail net- works being used for coal be used instead (as these will soon no longer be used for coal). Coal transportation infrastructure is widely available in Alberta because the extraction and distribution of fossil fuels such as coal and oil are the province's largest industries [39]. Using rail for pellet transportation (instead of truck) reduced the environmental impact by 76% (based on g CO2-e/dt in one study and 56% (based on g CO2-e/MJ electricity) in another; energy consumption was reduced by 77% (based on MJ/dtkm [8,116]. Transportation distance is assumed to be 200 km, which is the base distance in GREET for coal transportation to power generation plants [108]. This number is a reasonable assumption for the distance from the torrefaction plant to existing power plants in Alberta according to the estimated distance on a Google map. Also, the sensitivity of GHG emissions to both trans- portation distances (feedstock to torrefaction plant and bio-coal to power plant) is discussed later in the paper. However, the results of another study show that although transportation distance has a remarkably high effect on cost, it has little influence on GHG emissions [38]. Increasing the transportation distances of feedstock and biochar (from pyrolysis) from 15 km to 200 km increases GHG emissions only 5% [38]. Increasing the transportation distances even to 1000 km in- creases GHG emissions 28%.
As explained before, the energy intensity of diesel in heavy duty trucks is 1.41 in Alberta and the ratio of MJ non-renewable energy required per MJ diesel is 2.1 [82,109-111].

35: In this study, we assumed that the torrefaction plants are located between feedstock production/collection points and power plants to minimize the feedstock transportation distance rather than the bio-coal transportation distance. The feedstocks studied here are characterized by their nonhomogeneity, voluminosity, and high moisture content; these require a greater transportation effort than bio-coals, which are relatively dry, homogenous, and densified (due to the loss of volatiles during torrefaction) and impose less energy and environmental impacts.

Transportation involves loading, unloading, and trucking. Two transportation stages were considered: from the production/collection points to the torrefaction plants (for feedstock) and from the torrefaction plants to the power plant (for bio-coals). Heavy duty trucks and diesel were considered to transport the feedstocks to the torrefaction plants [108]. According to Natural Resources Canada data, the energy intensity or fuel efficiency of diesel in heavy duty trucks in Alberta is 1.41 MJ/t km [109]. It should be noted that a plant's geographical location plays a key role in transport energy intensities and transport efficiencies; the transport energy intensities of European countries are between 0.81 and 0.87 MJ/t km for a 40 tonne diesel truck [77]. The energy factor or non-renewable (non-ren) energy input to energy output for diesel is 1.2 MJ/MJ diesel [110,111]. This number can also be derived by dividing the energy density and the inherent energy of fuel. The energy density of diesel in Canada is 45.25 MJ non-ren/L diesel and its inherent energy is 38.64 MJ/L diesel [82,112]. In other geographical locations such as Australia, however, diesel energy density has been reported at 47.76 MJ non-ren/L diesel [112]. The diesel emission factor (including upstream production and distribution as well as combustion) is 88.78 g CO2-e/MJ diesel [113].

2.3.4.1. Wheat straw. The total transportation distance for wheat straw was calculated based on the method explained in other studies [84,104, 106,114]. It was assumed that plant is located in the center of the field (center of a circular area) where wheat straw is harvested. The area of land required to produce the required wheat straw (input to the torre- faction plant) was calculated using the average wheat straw yield of 0.63 dt/ha [84]. Using the area of the land or production field (circle), the radius of the circle was calculated which is the maximum distance that wheat straw is transported to the torrefaction plant from the center of the circle (field). The average biomass transportation distance or the average distance is 2/3 of the maximum radius [84]. But round-trip transportation should be considered to take into account driving the empty truck from the torrefaction plant to the field and the full truck to the plant. A tortuosity factor of 1.27 was applied to the transportation distance to take into account special geographical conditions such as hills, swamps, and lakes in the field [84].
The total roundtrip transportation distance for wheat straw is 59.56 km and the input primary energy is 100.78 MJ/t of wheat straw.

2.3.4.2. Pine woodchips. For pine woodchips, a transportation distance of 55.52 km (determined following the same method as for wheat straw) from the field to the torrefaction plant and 93.90 MJ energy/t residue were considered.

2.3.4.3. Grape pomace, manure and algae. No transportation distances were considered for grape pomace, manure, or algae, on the assumption that the torrefaction plants were located close to the feedstock produc- tion/collection points. If loading and transport of grape pomace is considered to be comparable to digestate, 14 MJ energy/dt is required for pomace loading and transportation [115]. This does not change the overall NER of grape pomace to electricity (via torrefaction); hence, neglecting the energy and environment impacts of preparation and loading pomace is justifiable.

It is assumed that road transportation (trucks) is used for trans- porting bio-coals to power plants. However, we suggest that rail net- works being used for coal be used instead (as these will soon no longer be used for coal). Coal transportation infrastructure is widely available in Alberta because the extraction and distribution of fossil fuels such as coal and oil are the province's largest industries [39]. Using rail for pellet transportation (instead of truck) reduced the environmental impact by 76% (based on g CO2-e/dt in one study and 56% (based on g CO2-e/MJ electricity) in another; energy consumption was reduced by 77% (based on MJ/dtkm [8,116]. Transportation distance is assumed to be 200 km, which is the base distance in GREET for coal transportation to power generation plants [108]. This number is a reasonable assumption for the distance from the torrefaction plant to existing power plants in Alberta according to the estimated distance on a Google map. Also, the sensitivity of GHG emissions to both trans- portation distances (feedstock to torrefaction plant and bio-coal to power plant) is discussed later in the paper. However, the results of another study show that although transportation distance has a remarkably high effect on cost, it has little influence on GHG emissions [38]. Increasing the transportation distances of feedstock and biochar (from pyrolysis) from 15 km to 200 km increases GHG emissions only 5% [38]. Increasing the transportation distances even to 1000 km in- creases GHG emissions 28%.
As explained before, the energy intensity of diesel in heavy duty trucks is 1.41 in Alberta and the ratio of MJ non-renewable energy required per MJ diesel is 2.1 [82,109-111].
36: The solid output of the processes, bio-coal (biochar and hydrochar), is the primary and only product. Bio-coals' properties and the two tor- refaction processes' characteristics have been described in detail in our previous study [11]. The feedstock properties and torrefaction pro- cesses' characteristics are shown in Table 1.
There is no credit assigned or impact allocated to co-products because none of the torrefaction processes has a significant co-product.

Wastewater (the output liquid stream) needs to be treated because of regulatory discharge limits, and the liquid output streams are sent to wastewater treatment facilities. There are uncertainties about whether to use process water as it may contain toxic metals or increase crop yields [10].

All output gas streams are directed into the combustor where it is assumed that they all are burned completely to produce CO2. Torgas, the gas output stream of DT, is burned with natural gas in the presence of air in the flare. The resulting high energy stream leaving the flare is directed to the dryer to satisfy dryer heat energy demand and dry the input biomass to the reactor.

There is very little gas phase and liquid phase (process water) data available. This lack of data has been reported in another study [34]. Overall, as DT and especially WT are new and not commercialized yet, there is insufficient knowledge on either technology [33]. In this study, it is assumed that wastewater is sent to a treatment facility and all the energy and emissions of its handling and treatment are outside the study's scope.

The environmental impacts associated with both torrefaction pro- cesses are from electricity, natural gas (used as torrefaction plant combustor fuel), and cooling water (used in the heat exchangers) re- quirements. Note that CO2 produced during this process is biogenic and
is therefore excluded from GHG emissions calculations.

The emissions generated during upstream production, handling, and transmission as well as natural gas combustion are included in this study. Overall, the natural gas emission factor is 77.35 CO2-e/MJ natural gas [113]. The electricity emission factor considered is this study is 702 g CO2-e/kW which has taken into account Alberta electricity mix and the associated emissions of the mix categories [117]. The cooling water emission factor was obtained from Miller and Kumar's study and is 0.93 g CO2-e/kg water [82].

The natural gas energy factor can be derived in the same way as diesel's using natural gas's inherent energy and energy density [112], or it can be directly obtained from the study by Cherubini et al. [110]. The natural gas energy factor is 1.2 MJ/MJ natural gas [112]. Electricity and cooling water energy factors are 9.89 MJ/kW h and 0.004 MJ/kg water, respectively [82].

39: Although the CO2 generated through bio-coal combustion in power plants is considered biogenic, there are some energy and environment impacts due to power plant operations and power generation. Very few available references have reported the amounts. Hondo [118] reported 6.7 CO2/kW h for the generation part of coal-fired power generation (which is in fact power plant operation-related emissions), while Ruether et al. stated 0.006 kg CO2-e/kW for power plant operation and maintenance (O&M) [119]. Although the two numbers are close, in this study, the latter is used. Both studies are from more than 10 years ago, hence, the lower amount is more justifiable as efficiencies are increasing and more environmentally conservative approaches have been placed in various sectors including the power generation sector. There is also little published data on power plant energy consumption. One study reported 0.026 MJ energy/kW electricity to be consumed through electricity generation operations alone [120].

Another GHG that can be produced when biomass-based materials are combusted is N2O (with significantly high global warming potential and formed during a complex set of reactions) [121]. Although it can react to produce N2O (which is a greenhouse gas), NOx is not a green- house gas and does not contribute to global warming potentials, yet it contributes to other environmental impact categories [122].

While CO2 emissions depend on fuel carbon content and efficiency, the N2O emission rate depends on many factors, mainly the process and its characteristics [123,124]. High combustion temperature and low excess air considerably decrease N2O emissions [121,125]. High-temperature pulverized coal and integrated gasification combined-cycle (IGCC) systems (unlike fluidized beds) typically operate at the mentioned conditions and hence their N2O emissions are negli- gible [119,126]. Based on Government of Canada information, Alberta's power generation from coal is primary focused on gasification and IGCC power generation is the preferred technology in Alberta. Moreover, the rise in electricity demand will be mainly met using IGCC technology [127, 128]. Considering all of the above, N2O emissions during bio-coal combustion in coal power plants is not included in this study.

The efficiencies of large coal and gas power plants for electricity generation vary depending on the type of coal and power plant tech- nology [129]. It is assumed that bio-coal conversion to electricity in existing power plants has the same efficiency as coal conversion.

Similar to power generation from coal, ash (bottom ash and fly ash) is produced during power generation from bio-coals. Based on the re- sults of some studies, the GHG emissions per kW h of electricity pro- duced during ash transportation to the landfill are negligible Some other studies, however, have included the environmental impact of ash disposal/use in their analysis and have discussed the impact of ash use in different applications such as cement manufacturing and earth construction on the environment [131,132]. In this study, emissions generated as a result of handling wastes produced in the power plant, including bottom ash and fly ash, are assumed to be outside the system boundary (as shown in Fig. 1).
Table 4 provides detailed inventory results of all the stages. Data for all stages were either calculated using the models developed in this study or extracted from previously published studies.
Table 5 provides detailed inventory results of all the stages of the feedstocks.

The energy efficiency of the system is evaluated using the net energy ratio (NER) as a metric. NER in this case is defined as the ratio of total energy output to total fossil energy consumed throughout the life cycle of the system [2,133].
NER = Energy, renewable energy produced Energy non-renewable primary energy input

For the GHG emissions estimations, the IPCC global warming po- tentials (GWPs) for a 100-year time perspective were considered which includes carbon dioxide (CO2):1, methane (CH4): 28 and nitrous oxide (N20): 265 [37].

43: Fig. 2(a) shows the energy consumption at each stage of the life cycle and the overall energy required for each pathway as well as associated bio-coal energy output. The pathways in which the energy output (the gray bar) is higher than the total energy input are energy efficient, and their associated NER is higher than 1. When the gray bar that presents energy output by bio-coal is higher than the overall input energy, the pathway is more energy efficient and the NER is higher than 1. More- over, when the overall consumed energy of the pathway is higher than output bio-coal energy, the pathway has lower energy efficiency and lower NER.
