Note that the input feedstock for all the pathways is 264 dt/day [11], which is significantly less than the amounts produced in Alberta [98,99]. At this capacity, economies of scale are achieved for the first time, and the unit capital cost does not decrease at higher capacities for a torrefaction plant [12].

of algae even though it has higher energy input requirements because it has a higher bio-coal energy output. Among the pathways that are en- ergy efficient, the DT of wheat straw and pine stands first. Overall, the energy requirement of torrefaction is the highest portion of consumed energy no matter the torrefaction process. In pathways with algae as feedstock, feedstock production is the second most energy-intensive life

cycle stage. In pathways with wheat straw feedstock, in-field prepara- tion is the most energy intensive. For all pathways, around 83% or more of the energy consumption occurs during the torrefaction stage except for the WT of algae at 50%, and DT of wheat straw at 54%.

Fig. 2(b) shows the share of energy at each stage of the life cycle of electricity production per unit of final product produced for each pathway. Energy consumption in the other stages is negligible except for algae, where feedstock production requires 10.38 MJ/kW and 13.04 MJ/kW h for DT and WT, respectively. Unlike other feedstocks, algae is not a waste, residue, or by-product, and its production requires nutrients, energy, and electricity. For wheat straw DT, in-field prepa- ration requires 0.72 MJ/kW h, which is significant because the total energy consumption for the torrefaction stage is 1.22 MJ/kW Of all the pathways, the torrefaction stage of wheat straw DT requires the lowest and algae DT the highest amount of energy (51.83 MJ/kW Overall, DT energy consumption is lower than WT's when feedstocks have low moisture content (i.e., wheat straw and pine). For the other three feedstocks, WT requires less energy than DT.

Fig. 3 presents the NER comparison of wet and dry torrefied biomass feedstocks. For lower moisture content feedstocks, DT is better and for higher moisture content WT is better. However, for very high moisture content feedstocks such as algae, none of the processes is acceptable from an NER point of view because of an NER < 1.

This means there is an optimum feedstock moisture content for WT energy efficiency, although for high moisture content feedstock, WT is still a better option than DT. Of the five feedstocks studied here, grape pomace (in the middle in terms of moisture content), has the highest NER for WT. So around 70% feedstock moisture content results in the highest NER for the WT process, and 20% and lower as well as 85% and higher moisture contents lead to NERs < 1 and an inefficient process in terms of energy. To come up with a specific optimum moisture content from an energy point of view for the WT process requires investigating more feedstocks with moisture contents in the range of 45-80%. How- ever, optimum moisture content is in the range of 45-80%. One key reason for the decreasing energy efficiency of WT plants that process very high moisture content feedstocks is the large amount of water that has to be dealt with. Water passes through heaters, flash separators and the reactor, changes temperature significantly, and turns into vapor and liquid. All of these require large amounts of energy, particularly when dealing with water with high specific heat, hence the energy con- sumption increases and NER decreases.

There is a decreasing trend between the energy efficiency of the DT process and feedstock moisture content. As the feedstock moisture content increases from 15% to 90%, the NER falls from 4.19 to 0.15. As its name indicates, the intrinsic property of the DT process is dry con- ditions, a dry process with dry feedstock. Thus, the feedstock has to be dried before entering the reactor in the dryer. Dryer energy consumption is an exponential function of feedstock moisture content [11] and drying is the most energy-intensive stage in the whole process [134]. This is the reason for decreasing DT NER with increasing moisture content. Overall, the results of the life cycle energy analysis indicate that electricity production from bio-coal has a net energy ratio of more than 1 in six pathways out of ten, meaning that more renewable energy is generated in these six systems than the fossil energy consumed.

Note that the input feedstock for all the pathways is 264 dt/day [11], which is significantly less than the amounts produced in Alberta [98,99]. At this capacity, economies of scale are achieved for the first time, and the unit capital cost does not decrease at higher capacities for a torrefaction plant [12].

of algae even though it has higher energy input requirements because it has a higher bio-coal energy output. Among the pathways that are en- ergy efficient, the DT of wheat straw and pine stands first. Overall, the energy requirement of torrefaction is the highest portion of consumed energy no matter the torrefaction process. In pathways with algae as feedstock, feedstock production is the second most energy-intensive life

cycle stage. In pathways with wheat straw feedstock, in-field prepara- tion is the most energy intensive. For all pathways, around 83% or more of the energy consumption occurs during the torrefaction stage except for the WT of algae at 50%, and DT of wheat straw at 54%.

Fig. 2(b) shows the share of energy at each stage of the life cycle of electricity production per unit of final product produced for each pathway. Energy consumption in the other stages is negligible except for algae, where feedstock production requires 10.38 MJ/kW and 13.04 MJ/kW h for DT and WT, respectively. Unlike other feedstocks, algae is not a waste, residue, or by-product, and its production requires nutrients, energy, and electricity. For wheat straw DT, in-field prepa- ration requires 0.72 MJ/kW h, which is significant because the total energy consumption for the torrefaction stage is 1.22 MJ/kW Of all the pathways, the torrefaction stage of wheat straw DT requires the lowest and algae DT the highest amount of energy (51.83 MJ/kW Overall, DT energy consumption is lower than WT's when feedstocks have low moisture content (i.e., wheat straw and pine). For the other three feedstocks, WT requires less energy than DT.

Fig. 3 presents the NER comparison of wet and dry torrefied biomass feedstocks. For lower moisture content feedstocks, DT is better and for higher moisture content WT is better. However, for very high moisture content feedstocks such as algae, none of the processes is acceptable from an NER point of view because of an NER < 1.

This means there is an optimum feedstock moisture content for WT energy efficiency, although for high moisture content feedstock, WT is still a better option than DT. Of the five feedstocks studied here, grape pomace (in the middle in terms of moisture content), has the highest NER for WT. So around 70% feedstock moisture content results in the highest NER for the WT process, and 20% and lower as well as 85% and higher moisture contents lead to NERs < 1 and an inefficient process in terms of energy. To come up with a specific optimum moisture content from an energy point of view for the WT process requires investigating more feedstocks with moisture contents in the range of 45-80%. How- ever, optimum moisture content is in the range of 45-80%. One key reason for the decreasing energy efficiency of WT plants that process very high moisture content feedstocks is the large amount of water that has to be dealt with. Water passes through heaters, flash separators and the reactor, changes temperature significantly, and turns into vapor and liquid. All of these require large amounts of energy, particularly when dealing with water with high specific heat, hence the energy con- sumption increases and NER decreases.

There is a decreasing trend between the energy efficiency of the DT process and feedstock moisture content. As the feedstock moisture content increases from 15% to 90%, the NER falls from 4.19 to 0.15. As its name indicates, the intrinsic property of the DT process is dry con- ditions, a dry process with dry feedstock. Thus, the feedstock has to be dried before entering the reactor in the dryer. Dryer energy consumption is an exponential function of feedstock moisture content [11] and drying is the most energy-intensive stage in the whole process [134]. This is the reason for decreasing DT NER with increasing moisture content. Overall, the results of the life cycle energy analysis indicate that electricity production from bio-coal has a net energy ratio of more than 1 in six pathways out of ten, meaning that more renewable energy is generated in these six systems than the fossil energy consumed.

57: Fig. 4(a) shows the CO2-e/yr emissions associated with each stage of life cycle of electricity production for each pathway. Except for algae WT and DT and wheat straw DT, the torrefaction process in both wet and dry conditions has the highest CO2-e emissions. However, as with energy consumption, feedstocks with lower moisture content (wheat straw and pine) have lower GHG emissions during DT than WT and feedstocks with higher moisture content have lower GHG emissions when torrefied in wet conditions. For wheat straw DT, in-field preparation has the largest share of total GHG emissions of this pathway, followed by the

Fig. 2. (a) Comparison of energy consumption for every stage of the life cycle and energy produced by bio-coal per year for each pathway (Note: Y-axis shows energy produced for 'Output bio-coal" and for all the other unit operations, it shows energy consumed') (b) Energy consumption per produced MJ/kW h electricity for each pathway and every stage of the life cycle.
Fig. 3. NER of electricity production pathways from various

torrefaction process. For algae, most of the GHG emissions are from feedstock production for both WT and DT. The torrefaction stage ac- counts for more than 84% of emitted GHGs except for algae WT, algae DT, and wheat straw DT at 10%, 36%, and 40%, respectively.

Note that as mentioned in previous sections, CO2 in the gas product and output gaseous streams is biogenic and so it does not contribute to the GHG emissions. The amount of carbon emissions from forest meta- bolism is considerably lower than the amount sequestered during tree growth under sustainable forest management practices. Regardless of power plant technology and coal type, fuel combustion in the power plant makes up about 95% of the life cycle carbon emissions [135]. That is why the biogenic CO2 plays an important role in the overall GHG emissions' reduction compared to coal, both during the torrefaction stage and power generation, and makes biomass-based (via WT and DT) electricity favorable.

The global warming potential for 1 kW h electricity produced from each feedstock is shown in Fig. 4(b) as g CO2-e/kW h. As Fig. 4(b) shows, replacing coal-derived electricity with bio-coal-derived electricity re- duces GHGs in all cases except for manure biochar. Electricity

Fig. 4. (a) CO2-e emissions of each life cycle stage of the different pathways, (b) CO2-e per kW h electricity produced for life cycle stages of each pathway and comparison with life cycle emissions of coal-based electricity.

production from algae-based hydrochar and biochar generates 8215.55 g CO2-e/kW h and 6544.09 g CO2-e/kW respectively, and SO algae is excluded in the bar chart for the sake of having other bars clearly showed. The largest positive impact on the GHG emissions is from electricity generation from pine biochar at 189.54 g CO2-e/kW h fol- lowed by wheat straw biochar at 212.10 g CO2-e/kW h and grape pomace hydrochar at 258.09 g CO2-e/kW h, while coal-derived and natural gas-derived electricity in Alberta leads to around 1041 g CO2-e/ kW h and 525 CO2-e/kW h life cycle emissions [136,137].

The largest contribution to GHG emissions in all pathways is made by the conversion processes (WT and DT) and the smallest contribution by biochar transportation to power plants. The composition of the elec- tricity mix is a key factor affecting the environmental performance of torrefaction processes because the largest part of imposed energy and emissions' burdens takes place during the process and is from the high electricity consumption. If Alberta's future mix includes cleaner energy sources, electricity generation from both hydrochars and biochars will produce fewer GHGs. The DT of very wet feedstocks such as manure and algae is not environmentally efficient and produces more GHGs than coal does.

3.2.1. Effects of using electricity produced from the combustion of biomass Alberta's grid electricity is the most fossilized in Canada and hence grid GHG emissions are the highest in the country [138]. Torrefaction plant operation requires a significant amount of electricity. Fig. 4(a) and (b) show that torrefaction processes' GHG emissions make up the highest portion of incurred CO2-e emissions during the whole life cycle of electricity generation from bio-coals of various biomass wastes. Electricity generation from biomass emits 46 g CO2-e/kW h [44].

Electricity production from direct combustion of biomass leads to low CO2-e emissions of 46 g CO2-e/kW h because the CO2 emissions from biomass combustion are considered biogenetic and because rela- tively little fossil fuel is used during the whole life cycle of electricity generation [44].

If the electricity used in a torrefaction plant is produced by the combustion of some of the biomass feedstock, CO2-e emissions per kW h of electricity decrease, as shown in Fig. 5. Using biomass to generate electricity leads to small decrease in GHG emissions in DT pathways. The reason is that the DT dryer uses natural gas and DT needs considerably less electricity than WT. GHG emissions decrease significantly in the WT pathways because of the high electricity consumption in the pumps, flash separators, and reactor (larger reactor in WT than in associated DT pathways). As Fig. 5 shows, life cycle GHG emissions of electricity generation from bio-coal in the four pathways are around or lower than 100 g CO2-e emissions/kW h.
Fig. 5. CO2-e per kW h electricity produced for life cycle stages of each pathway when the electricity consumed is produced from biomass combustion.
58: Fig. 4(a) shows the CO2-e/yr emissions associated with each stage of life cycle of electricity production for each pathway. Except for algae WT and DT and wheat straw DT, the torrefaction process in both wet and dry conditions has the highest CO2-e emissions. However, as with energy consumption, feedstocks with lower moisture content (wheat straw and pine) have lower GHG emissions during DT than WT and feedstocks with higher moisture content have lower GHG emissions when torrefied in wet conditions. For wheat straw DT, in-field preparation has the largest share of total GHG emissions of this pathway, followed by the

Fig. 2. (a) Comparison of energy consumption for every stage of the life cycle and energy produced by bio-coal per year for each pathway (Note: Y-axis shows energy produced for 'Output bio-coal" and for all the other unit operations, it shows energy consumed') (b) Energy consumption per produced MJ/kW h electricity for each pathway and every stage of the life cycle.
Fig. 3. NER of electricity production pathways from various

torrefaction process. For algae, most of the GHG emissions are from feedstock production for both WT and DT. The torrefaction stage ac- counts for more than 84% of emitted GHGs except for algae WT, algae DT, and wheat straw DT at 10%, 36%, and 40%, respectively.

Note that as mentioned in previous sections, CO2 in the gas product and output gaseous streams is biogenic and so it does not contribute to the GHG emissions. The amount of carbon emissions from forest meta- bolism is considerably lower than the amount sequestered during tree growth under sustainable forest management practices. Regardless of power plant technology and coal type, fuel combustion in the power plant makes up about 95% of the life cycle carbon emissions [135]. That is why the biogenic CO2 plays an important role in the overall GHG emissions' reduction compared to coal, both during the torrefaction stage and power generation, and makes biomass-based (via WT and DT) electricity favorable.

The global warming potential for 1 kW h electricity produced from each feedstock is shown in Fig. 4(b) as g CO2-e/kW h. As Fig. 4(b) shows, replacing coal-derived electricity with bio-coal-derived electricity re- duces GHGs in all cases except for manure biochar. Electricity

Fig. 4. (a) CO2-e emissions of each life cycle stage of the different pathways, (b) CO2-e per kW h electricity produced for life cycle stages of each pathway and comparison with life cycle emissions of coal-based electricity.

production from algae-based hydrochar and biochar generates 8215.55 g CO2-e/kW h and 6544.09 g CO2-e/kW respectively, and SO algae is excluded in the bar chart for the sake of having other bars clearly showed. The largest positive impact on the GHG emissions is from electricity generation from pine biochar at 189.54 g CO2-e/kW h fol- lowed by wheat straw biochar at 212.10 g CO2-e/kW h and grape pomace hydrochar at 258.09 g CO2-e/kW h, while coal-derived and natural gas-derived electricity in Alberta leads to around 1041 g CO2-e/ kW h and 525 CO2-e/kW h life cycle emissions [136,137].

The largest contribution to GHG emissions in all pathways is made by the conversion processes (WT and DT) and the smallest contribution by biochar transportation to power plants. The composition of the elec- tricity mix is a key factor affecting the environmental performance of torrefaction processes because the largest part of imposed energy and emissions' burdens takes place during the process and is from the high electricity consumption. If Alberta's future mix includes cleaner energy sources, electricity generation from both hydrochars and biochars will produce fewer GHGs. The DT of very wet feedstocks such as manure and algae is not environmentally efficient and produces more GHGs than coal does.

3.2.1. Effects of using electricity produced from the combustion of biomass Alberta's grid electricity is the most fossilized in Canada and hence grid GHG emissions are the highest in the country [138]. Torrefaction plant operation requires a significant amount of electricity. Fig. 4(a) and (b) show that torrefaction processes' GHG emissions make up the highest portion of incurred CO2-e emissions during the whole life cycle of electricity generation from bio-coals of various biomass wastes. Electricity generation from biomass emits 46 g CO2-e/kW h [44].

Electricity production from direct combustion of biomass leads to low CO2-e emissions of 46 g CO2-e/kW h because the CO2 emissions from biomass combustion are considered biogenetic and because rela- tively little fossil fuel is used during the whole life cycle of electricity generation [44].

If the electricity used in a torrefaction plant is produced by the combustion of some of the biomass feedstock, CO2-e emissions per kW h of electricity decrease, as shown in Fig. 5. Using biomass to generate electricity leads to small decrease in GHG emissions in DT pathways. The reason is that the DT dryer uses natural gas and DT needs considerably less electricity than WT. GHG emissions decrease significantly in the WT pathways because of the high electricity consumption in the pumps, flash separators, and reactor (larger reactor in WT than in associated DT pathways). As Fig. 5 shows, life cycle GHG emissions of electricity generation from bio-coal in the four pathways are around or lower than 100 g CO2-e emissions/kW h.
Fig. 5. CO2-e per kW h electricity produced for life cycle stages of each pathway when the electricity consumed is produced from biomass combustion.

64: Sensitivity analysis was conducted to study the impact of using different allocation methods on the overall GHG emissions of electricity generation from wheat straw biochar and hydrochar. The emissions generated as a result of the production (cultivation and harvesting) of co-products (grain and straw) are allocated on three bases (mass, en- ergy, and market value) and the results are compared in Table 6. As the table shows, the lowest life cycle emissions belong to the case in which emissions are allocated based on market values. This is because the market value of wheat straw is considerably lower than wheat grain in Canada [11,139]. Hence, fewer emissions will be allocated to wheat straw than in the mass and energy allocation methods. These two methods have relatively similar allocation factors because wheat straw and wheat grain have very close LHVs as shown in Table 6 [83]. The results show a remarkably high difference of about 154 g CO2-e/kW h over the whole life cycle between allocation methods. This highlights the importance of allocation method selection, which can significantly affect the GHG emissions assessment results. The results also indicate that the production emissions impact is lower in the hydrochar-based electricity generation pathway than biochar-based compared to the base values (Fig. 4) because of the lower overall GHG emissions of the biochar-based pathway.

3.3.2. Transportation distances to torrefaction plant and to power plant Sensitivity analysis was conducted to study the effect of two trans- portation distances on the GHG emissions during transportation stages. The two stages of transportation are the transportation of the feedstock to the torrefaction plant and the transportation of bio-coal from the torrefaction plant to the power plant.

Because the bio-coal of the DT of pine for electricity generation generates fewer GHGs (of all the pathways) than coal, in this section, the effect of changing transportation distances on CO2-e of dry torrefied pine-based electricity was studied. Fig. 6(a) and (b) show the results of the sensitivity of g CO2-e emissions per kW h electricity produced during transportation to transportation distances.

As is clear from Fig. 6(a), emissions are more sensitive to changes in transportation distance from the field to the torrefaction plant than from the torrefaction plant to the power plant. This means that increasing the distance between field and plant generates more GHGs than increasing the distance between torrefaction plant and power plant by the same amount. Compared to adding 1 km in bio-coal transportation, adding 1 km in feedstock transportation adds 2.25 times more GHGs emissions.
Fig. 6(b) shows the difference in GHG emissions in the transportation of feedstocks and bio-coal if the distances for feedstock transportation
65: Sensitivity analysis was conducted to study the impact of using different allocation methods on the overall GHG emissions of electricity generation from wheat straw biochar and hydrochar. The emissions generated as a result of the production (cultivation and harvesting) of co-products (grain and straw) are allocated on three bases (mass, en- ergy, and market value) and the results are compared in Table 6. As the table shows, the lowest life cycle emissions belong to the case in which emissions are allocated based on market values. This is because the market value of wheat straw is considerably lower than wheat grain in Canada [11,139]. Hence, fewer emissions will be allocated to wheat straw than in the mass and energy allocation methods. These two methods have relatively similar allocation factors because wheat straw and wheat grain have very close LHVs as shown in Table 6 [83]. The results show a remarkably high difference of about 154 g CO2-e/kW h over the whole life cycle between allocation methods. This highlights the importance of allocation method selection, which can significantly affect the GHG emissions assessment results. The results also indicate that the production emissions impact is lower in the hydrochar-based electricity generation pathway than biochar-based compared to the base values (Fig. 4) because of the lower overall GHG emissions of the biochar-based pathway.

71: As mentioned above, Alberta's grid electricity emission factor is very high (more than 700 g CO2-e/kW h). Fig. 6(c) and (d) show the effect of changes in the electricity emission factors on the life cycle GHG

emissions of electricity generation from bio-coals produced from pine and manure, respectively. Using electricity with 50% fewer emissions (351 g CO2-e/kW h electricity) reduces life cycle GHG emissions by 8% and 46% from pine-based biochar and manure-based hydrochar, respectively.
72: As natural gas is another fossil-based source of energy used in bio- coal-based electricity generation, it is important to study the effect of natural gas consumption on GHG emissions. The results are shown in Fig. 6(e) and (f). As the figures illustrate clearly, the effect of natural gas consumption on life cycle CO2-e emissions and CO2-e/kW h electricity is not very high, for two reasons: the lower emission factor of natural gas
compared to that of Alberta's electricity grid and the lower consumption requirements for natural gas than for electricity in the pathways.
73: Life cycle energy and emissions of electricity production from bio- coals produced via wet and dry torrefaction of five different feed- stocks were assessed. Torrefaction process is the most influential life cycle stage on both energy ratio and GHG emissions in every pathway except wheat straw dry torrefaction (DT) and algae DT and wet torre- faction (WT). More GHGs are emitted during the in-field preparation of wheat straw and algae production than in torrefaction. From an energy point of view, DT is considerably superior for wheat straw and pine woodchips and WT for higher moisture content feedstocks. Pine biochar reduces GHG emissions by 82% compared to coal. The results also show that biomass combustion for generating plant required electricity rather than using grid electricity decreases GHG emissions in all pathways particularly in WT pathways due to the high electricity consumption during wet torrefaction process. Emissions allocation for wheat straw and wheat grain based on the market value leads to the lowest GHG emissions compared to mass and energy allocations. Changes in trans- portation distance from the field to the torrefaction plant has higher environmental impact compared to that of the torrefaction plant to the power plant due to higher density of feedstocks compared to bio-coals. Reducing grid emissions to half, reduces life cycle GHG emission of all pathways, particularly WT pathways due to their higher electricity requirements.
